rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE MELBA S. MARAVILLA REVOCABLE LIVING TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Leave To File Second Amended Petition; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities With Appendix Of Proposed Changes; Declaration Of Paul H. Melbostad; And [Proposed] Order
rulings.Rulings: Petitioner's Motion for Leave to File Second Amended Petition is granted. Petitioner is seeking leave to amend her petition challenging the validity of the Melba S. Maravilla Revocable Trust to augment her standing to sue with citation to Barefoot v. Jennings (2020) 8 Cal. 5th 822. Respondents oppose the motion on grounds that Petitioner does not have standing to challenge the Maravilla Trust, regardless of the holding in Barefoot. The California Supreme Court's opinion in Barefoot is not yet one-year old, and its impact on probate trust litigation standing issues as controlling precedent is still developing. Petitioner's theory of standing based on Barefoot is sufficiently novel that, "the preferable practice would be to permit the amendment and allow the parties to test its legal sufficiency by demurrer, motion for judgment on the pleadings or other appropriate proceedings." (California Cas. Gen. Ins. Co. v. Sup. Ct. (1985) 173 Cal. App. 3d 274, 281.) There is no showing that Respondents would be substantially prejudiced by the proposed amendments. Petitioner also seeks leave to add material to the existing financial elder abuse claim brought under Welfare and Institutions Code sections 15600-15675. Respondents did not oppose the proposed additions in their opposition to this motion. There is no showing these proposed additions would prejudice the Respondents or that the additional claims are time-barred. Therefore, the amendment augmenting the financial elder abuse claim will also be permitted. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on November 10, 2020. Remote appearance is strongly encouraged, in conformity with the current General Order of the Probate and Mental Health Division, available on the court's website at https://sfsuperiorcourt.org/divisions/probate.
